speaker: interviewer
text: Doing everything to get your husband out.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Soon after, I think it was on the 24th of November, when I got a call from Hugo from Dusseldorf. He had been released and he came to Berlin on the 25th. In the meantime, I found out that people who had come back from concentration camps had to report with the Gestapo. And when they said it was good, they were sent back to the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were interviewed by the Gestapo in Berlin and they asked, how was it? Was it good, was it bad? And they would say, oh, it was OK. Then they sent them back to the concentration camp. When the people said it was horrible, they were sent back to the concentration camp because they were not supposed to say anything. They were told that when they are being questioned, that they shall say, I am committed to silence and that is all. So these people-- and some of them-- How did you learn this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned that from people who had been there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before Hugo came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, before Hugo came back. So when Hugo came back, I told him-- by the way, I do want to say that Hugo did not look like Hugo. he had lost a tremendous lot of weight. He was as thin as a stick. His hair was gone. He was so quiet. He couldn't talk. He could not even tell me how it was. He was so overwhelmed and so shocked that he couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was he there?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was there from the 20th of November to the 24th of December.

speaker: interviewer
text: About a month.

speaker: interviewee
text: A little over a month. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he'd lost so much weight.

speaker: interviewee
text: He'd lost a tremendous lot of weight. In fact, his father told me later that he was ready to kill himself on the electric fence and his father had to hold him back. He was so distraught. His father, by the way, was released before Hugo. Why? Because he had a big store, a jewelry store, and the Nazis in Dusseldorf didn't know anything about jewelry so they needed him to educate them what kind of jewelry it was for them to take it away. But my husband had nothing, he didn't even have a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So therefore, it was more difficult for him to get out. So anyhow, when he then came home, I advised him to say he is committed to silence and he cannot speak about it, which he did. He never had to go back until he was telling them, when do you think you will leave Germany? We had nothing, absolutely nothing-- no family, no friends-- nothing. We didn't even know whether we would be able to migrate to anywhere. But you had the tickets?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ticket for England, which was absolutely worthless. I couldn't go to England because the English government would not accept me. So what happened in the meantime, my sister, Dora, and Walter saw the handwriting on the wall. They sold everything and took a pleasure trip to Shanghai and got out that way. Shortly after, even Shanghai closed the doors. The doors were closed all over the world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sister--

speaker: interviewee
text: We couldn't get anywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your sister remained in Shanghai during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until-- then a cousin of mine, Mr. [GERMAN], sponsored her and she came and her husband came then to America. And these people, I told you I took over the job, settled in San Bernardino. They had a grocery store. Not long they left again for Los Angeles. But they kept in contact with my sister and my sister came to San Bernardino to live. And that's how we are here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyhow, when my husband then came home, we had nowhere to go. I had my ears and eyes open all the time and I found out that the English government gave the Kitchener Camp, which before was a prison camp, to divert it for people who had to get out of Germany who were in concentration camps. So I contacted the Jewish community, the same community. I couldn't get ahead. Which Jewish community were you contacting?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin. I could not get to them and finally, I realized why. All the employees in that Jewish community had big families and had families who also were taken to concentration camp. So they wanted to cover them. So I found out the name of the top man and I called and I said, I want to speak to my cousin, Mr. So-and-So, and they finally gave me an appointment. I got up there--

speaker: interviewer
text: He wasn't really your cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But I had to realize it was a matter of do or die. So I spoke to him and I said, look, you did not get them out of the concentration camp, I got them out. But you better get them out of Germany. I insist that you take his name and you get him out. He was taken on the list and sure enough, he got permission to go to England in April of '39. One day after he left, the Gestapo came to my house, my parents' home, and said that they are looking for my husband. I said, my husband is already in England. So he said, you thank your God because I had instructions to take him back to a concentration camp. And he left.

speaker: interviewer
text: He came to your house because--

speaker: interviewee
text: He came to my parents' home and he left. We never saw me again.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you feeling about all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a horrible feeling. It was a terrible feeling. Now I got him out. It was the most important thing for him to get out because I realized if I don't get him out, nobody will get him out. And thank God I was capable and healthy and had a brain to see what I can do with it, and I did. I was young then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you've got yourself and your baby and your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I had me and my baby, nowhere to go-- nowhere to go. All the doors were closed. In the meantime, America opened up the doors. We had to go and sign in and try to get an affidavit. Finally, I got a cousin of mine, the same cousin who helped my sister get out of Shanghai. She was giving us an affidavit. But I had to register, and for two nights and three days I had to line up to sign in at the American embassy to have my name there. Because otherwise, they would not let me go out. Now-- Was your mother taking care of the baby? My-- yes. While I lined up, yes. She had to take care of the baby. And in the meantime, I still felt I don't know where or what. But I found out that a big pharmacist-- pharmaceutical company in Milan was coming to Berlin on a business trip and he stayed at the [INAUDIBLE] Hotel. So I called him and asked him if he would allow me to see him. What I hoped we could do that he could sponsor me for Italy with Sonia, with my baby, and then from Italy go to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hear about this man?

speaker: interviewee
text: This man, he was a customer of ours from at the [GERMAN] and I corresponded with all these people. And I found out he wrote that he is going to be in Berlin at such and such a time, at such and such a hotel, and he will call us. And we were making arrangements for him to see us. It was a business arrangement.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went. I didn't want the conversation to be in our office. And I went to see him. And after I talked to him, he says, you don't have to come to Italy. I have a daughter who lives in London and I will see that she gets you into London. In the meantime-- Had you known this man before or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. Never really knew him personally, only through business. I corresponded with him as the secretary of the [GERMAN]. I never saw him, I never met him. But he was a very fine, kind man. I didn't ask him his religion. I didn't dare ask him. And he said, my daughter lives in London. I'm going to write to her or call her and see what she can do. Now, I got word in order for me to enter into England, I have to make a test to be a cleaning woman. So they gave me a big house, a two-story house, that I had to clean from A to Z and then professional cleaners would come in and see if I did a good job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who set this up?

speaker: interviewee
text: That set-- the-- the Jewish community set that up. They-- they-- I don't know what they got in touch with, but they set it up for me to clean that house. So I get the permission to get a house cleaning job.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was a test?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a test.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you ever cleaned a house?

speaker: interviewee
text: My own house, but not anybody else's. And so I got an A for my test. So that was good. She found out that Professor Norman Bentwich, who was the head of the Kitchener Camp and had a summer cottage in Sandwich, he lived in London but he had to go to supervise now and then to Sandwich. And he needed a housekeeper in his cottage in Sandwich, was not far from the Kitchener Camp so I got the job. And I was able to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was all done by letters?

speaker: interviewee
text: By letters and telephone. I never spoke to Professor Bentwich, but she made all the arrangements and my husband was allowed to pick me up. Now, I had to pack everything up and was only allowed to take 10 marks out of Germany. I had 150 marks. So every station we stopped, they thought that we would not be able to go through because there was the warning of a war. And if a war would have been at that time, we would not have been able to get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date was the 18th of August. The 18th of August. And the war broke out on the 3rd.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you going by train?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train, to the Belgian border by train. And each time they made a stop--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was on the train with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only my baby. and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents see you off?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They couldn't get out. You had to have--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I mean say goodbye to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. They came to say goodbye to me at the train station. And that was a horrible thing. It was a horrible torture because we didn't know whether we would ever see each other again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Never. So I got--

speaker: interviewer
text: So then tell us about the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I got-- and I had $150 worth me. And just before-- and every time in Dusseldorf we had to all leave the train, and I had a little lunch with my baby. And naturally, she was crying. She was still a 16-month-old. And so we met an English couple in the [INAUDIBLE]. And we spoke and she found out who I was and that I'm going to England. And then when we went back into the train I didn't see her any more.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as we approached Belgium, we got over Belgium and the first big-- it was a coffee shop or something, I-- oh, before we left for Belgium I put $150 in an envelope and gave it to a stranger on the train station before we stopped before Belgium, one city-- I don't remember the name. My parents got the money. I gave it to a stranger and said, would you please mail this letter? And he did and they got the money. So I was here with 10 marks in Belgium. Suddenly over the loudspeaker, Mrs. Blum, Mrs. Blum is wanted. My heart fell. I didn't know. I didn't know any-- But you were already in Belgium.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was already Belgium. I didn't know anybody. I thought, oh my God. If they want me to go back or something, I don't know-- or something happened. So finally I went onto the telephone and they told me that this couple that I met in Dusseldorf invited me for breakfast with my baby. So I had breakfast with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you-- were you carrying the baby, or was she--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a stroller. I had a stroller. And so then we boarded the boat. And they were very nice. Because I had the baby, they let me come in first. And then also I already spoke a little English. I was able to converse with some people and that came very handy for the people that spoke English.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I could sort of translate a little bit. And then I arrived in Dover and my husband picked me up in Dover. And we went then to Sandwich. So when he was at the Kitchener Camp, that means he was free to come and go.

speaker: interviewee
text: With permission. With permission. When he told them that he's expecting me and wants to pick me up from Dover, they let him go. Yes, they did. So then he took me to Sandwich. It was a beautiful, beautiful cottage-- very small. It had one bedroom, one dining room, one living room, and an extra room for the maid. And that was my room.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the baby.

speaker: interviewee
text: With the baby. Now, they instructed me that I had to cook and to clean. And I got three shillings a week, which was nothing. But to me, three shillings was a lot of money. They had a lot of food there which I didn't touch, it was not mine. But Hugo brought a lot of food from the Kitchener Camp because he started to come in and eat in my house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was allowed to take certain portions of food and he took always a little bit more so I and the baby would have. So we managed. What happened through your-- you weren't allowed to be able to keep kosher in this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't, no way. There was no-- I didn't have any pork or ham or bacon, no. And I didn't mix milk and meat, I didn't. But I could not keep kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did the best you could.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did the best I could. Now I'm being informed that Professor Norman Bentwich is coming the next week and he is bringing a lord and a lady and for me to prepare a very festive dinner. And I prepared a turkey.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you a good cook?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I was a good cook. I must say that. I cooked internationally. I always learned from other people. And I'll never forget, we never knew chestnuts and I found out the American people love chestnuts. So I made the turkey with chestnuts and the trimmings. And it was on a Friday night and I had two candlesticks and I had two candles and they were sitting there. So the professor came out and he said, would you like to make the [HEBREW]. He was Jewish. He was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't know that until that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. And he said, would you like to-- would you like to make the [HEBREW]? I said, I'd be delighted. And I went in the dining room, made the [HEBREW], and walked out. And I was busy in the kitchen. And after the dinner he came out and he said, the dinner was absolutely delicious, was beautiful. And I was happy. And I had to give Sonia to a babysitter because she was too small for me to handle, you know in cooking and everything. And I cleaned everything up and in the morning, I'll never forgot-- and that is rather comical and I like to say that-- you know, I pretended that these people are my guests and I had a very elegant dressing gown. And they have tea and cookies in the morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had baked cookies and I had the tea on the tray and I went up to bring them the tea and the cookies and to the lord and the lady. And they said, in future, you come in your uniform, not in your dressing gown. So I didn't have a uniform, but I had just an ordinary regular dress and I did. So anyhow, I worked for them. While they were gone, I invited all the men from the Kitchener Camp, the Jewish men that were alone-- some of them brought their wives along and the wives were living in Sandwich, but they didn't-- only one couple, the wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was Jewish and his wife was Christian and she worked for a minister in his home. But the others did not have wives. And I used to make every Friday at night at dinner for all these people so they have a little home dinner and a home atmosphere. I was allowed to do that when they were not coming. So I always had a nice group of people and gave them the feeling of a home atmosphere. They were very grateful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell me what you learned about the Kitchener Camp. How many people were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a lot. I think I'll give you a picture. There were a lot of men there, only men.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many? Did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple of hundred-- 300, 400.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody had to work. My husband was assigned to the kitchen. He had to do kitchen work. Some were assigned to cleaning the barracks, some of them were assigned to clean the outside, the streets. Everybody had a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where had they come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: They come from all over Germany, all over who had been in concentration camps and released and had a place to go there from and couldn't wait in Berlin for them to go to a foreign country. In the meantime, America opened the door, but we had to wait until we get our visa. And that happened to a lot of them. I will say this, that I only took clothing and things to England only for six weeks because we were told that we would be able to leave England after six weeks, our visa would be issued in London. So all my stuff-- china and everything-- I sent to America to my brother-in-law and he kept it. We only had clothes for six weeks, that's all. We were only supposed to be there six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me a little bit more about Kitchener Camp. Did it have anything to do with Lord Kitchener?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was Lord Kitchener for prisoners. He used to have the prisoners there in World War I. And it was empty and vacant so the government of England felt, why can't we give it for temporary housing for the Jewish men who had to leave London-- Berlin?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all Jewish men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only Jewish people. Now, in the meantime, there was a Jewish man in the Kitchener Camp that recognized a Gestapo from his concentration camp. He immediately went to the police without telling anybody and reported.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where he recognize this Gestapo agent?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Kitchener Camp. That man was in the Kitchener Camp. He spoke perfect German, and most people in the Kitchener Camp were Germans. So he recognized him to be one of the camp Nazis. He was a big official in the concentration camp and he recognized him. I don't know his name, I don't know anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? He went and immediately reported to the police. The Scotland Yard came in and they found this man plus two or three others who had dropped from an aeroplane in the middle of the night to infiltrate, to spy on England. Immediately, my husband was picked up from my home in Sandwich, taken to the Kitchener camp. We were not told why, we were not told-- Who picked up Hugo?

speaker: interviewee
text: The police.

speaker: interviewer
text: Local police.

speaker: interviewee
text: The local police picked him up and he was taken back to the Kitchener Camp. I went with my baby to the Kitchener Camp. I was not allowed to enter. The next day, all of them were gone. We were not informed where they were gone, why they were gone, what happened-- nothing. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: It must have been terrifying.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was. It was. So what happened, in the meantime the Germans had started to come halfway up the channel to invade England, where they were repulsed. But we had to leave Sandwich at once, from one day to the next. I had to pack everything up and we were horded on trains to London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who is we?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the Jewish women who lived in Sandwich, their husbands being in the Kitchener Camp. I and my baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many other women did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I only knew two or three.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you knew there were more?

speaker: interviewee
text: There might have been more. Well, they collected them from all over England, you know, so I really don't know. I only know we were taken then to London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The police and a Jewish from the Jewish Committee from London.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were taking you to protect you.

speaker: interviewee
text: To protect us to London. And we all had to assemble in a hall. It was 120 people were sleeping in one room. And I had my baby with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other babies?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was only another baby, but mine was there. And my baby had an extra cot. The next day, the Scotland Yard came in and interviewed every one of us. When it came my turn and I was interviewed by the Scotland Yard, they wanted to know who we were, whether we spoke German. They wanted to know who we were. I walked out and to be interviewed. In the meantime, one of the helpers called me that my baby is crying, I should come right away. I went, took my baby to the bathroom, and left my purse on my bed while I took my baby to the bathroom. When I came back, my purse and my identification was gone.
